items.question: Write the formulas for MSE, MAE, BCE, and one-hot CE on a single example.
items.answer: MSE: (y − ŷ)². MAE: |y − ŷ|. BCE: −y log ŷ − (1−y) log(1−ŷ). CE (one-hot class y): −log ŷ_y where ŷ is softmax over classes.

items.question: Why is minimizing average cross-entropy equivalent to maximum likelihood?
items.answer: Average CE = (1/n) Σ −log q_θ(y_i) = negative average log-likelihood under Bernoulli/Categorical Q_θ. Maximizing likelihood minimizes CE.

items.question: When would you choose MAE over MSE for training?
items.answer: When outliers or label noise should not dominate — MAE gives linear penalty per unit error instead of quadratic. Trade-off: MAE has a non-smooth corner at zero error.

items.question: Why should you not use MSE with one-hot class targets?
items.answer: MSE treats classes as real numbers and does not correspond to Bernoulli/Categorical likelihood. Gradients are weak and predictions are not proper probabilities. Use BCE or CE instead.

items.question: How does BCE relate to H(P, Q) in the Cross-entropy topic?
items.answer: BCE is H(P, Q) when P is the Bernoulli label distribution and Q is the sigmoid prediction. The general H(P, Q) = −Σ p log q definition lives in Cross-entropy; this topic applies the Bernoulli and one-hot specializations for training.
